data.title: Commodities: The U.S. Scrambles To Rein In Oil Prices
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy: Refined product volatility

ICE Brent settled above US$85/bbl yesterday, up 4.9%, the highest close since the Middle East conflict began. The market remains well supported with few signs of de-escalation in the Middle East and a resumption of energy flows in the region. Clearly, with every day that goes by without flows resuming, the oil market will reprice the amount of supply lost, leaving room for prices to move higher.

However, in early trading in Asia today, prices gave back some of yesterday’s gains. This follows the US's announcement that it would allow the sale of Russian oil to India for 30 days. The waiver allows the sale of Russian crude and products to India that were loaded onto vessels before 5 March and remains valid until 4 April.

This essentially allows floating Russian oil to reach India. According to Bloomberg, there are at least 9.5m barrels of Russian oil floating in Asian waters. While this might help put some immediate downward pressure on the market, it is not a game-changer. The only way for prices to come down on a sustained basis is a resumption of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

This action by the US is part of the administration's plan to try to cap oil prices. The US is also considering tapping the strategic petroleum reserve (SPR), which currently sits at 415m barrels. If the US government were comfortable drawing down the SPR to the levels seen in 2023, we could expect a release of around 68m barrels.

Refined product markets remain volatile amid disruptions in the Persian Gulf. Jet fuel cracks have seen the biggest moves amid concerns over jet fuel supply from the region. Around 23% of the global seaborne jet fuel trade moves through the Strait of Hormuz.

In addition, refiners in other regions facing disruptions in crude supply will be forced to reduce run rates. This will further tighten the jet fuel and broader refined-product markets. It is difficult to see the recently elevated levels and volatility in cracks disappear until there are signs of an imminent resumption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

China telling refiners to stop exporting diesel and gasoline added support to refined product cracks yesterday amid ongoing supply concerns. China exported 8m tonnes of gasoline and 6.7m tonnes of diesel in 2025.

The risk is that other countries take similar action. It would not be unreasonable to expect India to make a similar move, given its track record of taking protectionist measures amid supply concerns (though more so in food and agri markets). This would provide further upside to middle distillates.

Metals: Copper slips amid inventory surge

Copper prices softened on Thursday as a sharp rise in exchange inventories reinforced concerns that supply is outpacing demand. LME copper fell around 1.2% to settle near $12,900/t, while stocks tracked by the exchange jumped almost 8% on the day to a 16‑month high.

The inventory build reflects strong inflows into LME warehouses, driven by shifting regional pricing incentives. LME copper has been trading at only a narrow premium to Comex, reversing last year’s structure that encouraged metal to flow into US warehouses. As these pricing signals normalise, metal is increasingly being redirected back into global exchange stocks.

The inventory surge creates a tougher near‑term backdrop for prices. The key things to watch are whether LME stocks continue to build at this pace, the evolution of LME–Comex spreads, and signs of restocking from China once price pressure eases—all of which will determine whether copper can stabilise or remains vulnerable to further consolidation.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Aflac: The Richest Duck In The World
data.description:
When I was a kid, my grandmother used to give us a comic book we called Super Picsou Géant - compilations of stories starring Donald Duck, Mickey Mouse, and Uncle Scrooge (Oncle Picsou in French). That's where it started. I didn't see Scrooge as just a cartoon character. There was something else there. He felt obsessive, methodical, adventurous, fearless, and resourceful.

In the 1990s, DuckTales was on television, telling the adventures of Uncle Scrooge and his three nephews - Huey, Dewey, and Louie (Riri, Fifi, and Loulou - still in French). To me it wasn't just a children's show. It was a recurring story about wealth and patience, with a hint of adventure.

At some point - almost without realizing it - I started counting my own money. I kept a small, homemade notebook; not really a notebook, just a bunch of loose pages I had glued together myself. On those pages, I drew a chart with a ruler and a pencil, carefully plotting every new coin I earned from small jobs I did for my parents or my grandmother. Back then, it was French francs, which tells you how long ago this was.

I've since lost that little notebook, but I still remember the graph - the slow, uneven climb of savings traced in pencil. Around that same time, my father, who was a banker - and a former math teacher, explained to me the concept of compound interest. I must have been somewhere between eight and twelve years old. That lesson remained. Along with what the French call "les identités remarquables" (e.g., (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²) - simple formulas, learned early, that quietly structure everything.

Twenty years later, I am an actuary, dealing with reserves, mostly using simple approaches to forecast ultimate claim amounts. But something has remained unchanged: Scrooge McDuck remains my favorite comics character, and I still read stories written by Don Rosa and Carl Barks.

Why are you babbling about Scrooge McDuck? Because, I believe it's a fair comparison to Aflac (AFL). The Duck is not a growth story; it is a discipline story. The company operates a simple supplemental insurance model, with a dominant presence in Japan. The insurance carrier has delivered steady underwriting margins over time in both U.S. and Japanese markets. Thanks to this, the company, aware of its own limitations, has returned excess capital to shareholders through gradually increasing dividends and active share repurchases.

While demographic trends in Japan and currency exposure remain risks, the company's steady underwriting profitability and straightforward capital allocation policy support a long-term compounding profile.

Impenetrable Like A Bank Safe

Like Huey, Dewey, and Louie, Aflac's history is related to three brothers: John, Paul & Bill Amos. The company started to be a success story when the business expanded into Japan. Aflac is well-positioned in Japan, but I am not impressed by the company's commercial momentum or growth. I am impressed by its underwriting resilience. My fellow followers - and readers - know how I am focused (obsessed?) on the underwriting expertise; Aflac is not a traditional property and casualty (P&C) insurance company. Still, the benefit ratio is similar to the loss ratio, and a combined ratio can be calculated.

When I covered Aflac, in 2018, the overall over-cycle underwriting margin was about 5-7%, or a combined ratio of circa 93-95%. Since then, the underwriting performance has oscillated between 5 and 10%.

2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019
Japan 11.0% 7.7% 3.7% 5.8% 4.4% 5.6%
US 8.7% 9.7% 7.3% 10.7% 8.0% 9.1%
Total 10.0% 8.5% 5.0% 7.4% 5.6% 6.7%

The underwriting performance for the period 2019-2024 was broadly similar to the pre-2018 period, with a lower volatility in the U.S. than in Japan. In 2025, the underwriting performance was 10.5% (8.6% in the U.S. and 12.3% in Japan)

Japan 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019
Expense Ratio 28.4% 26.7% 26.3% 27.5% 27.1% 25.7% 24.9%
Benefit Ratio 59.3% 62.3% 66.0% 68.8% 67.2% 69.9% 69.5%
Combined Ratio 87.7% 89.0% 92.3% 96.3% 94.2% 95.6% 94.4%
U.S. 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019
Expense Ratio 44.1% 44.5% 47.4% 48.8% 45.7% 44.0% 41.4%
Benefit Ratio 47.3% 46.8% 42.8% 43.8% 43.6% 48.0% 49.4%
Combined Ratio 91.4% 91.3% 90.3% 92.7% 89.3% 92.0% 90.9%

Although the Japanese market is declining (¥1.18 trillion of annualized premiums in 2025 vs. ¥1.49 trillion in 2019 and ¥1.53 trillion in 2018), the Japanese branch remains the company's stronghold and has consistently delivered steady and recurring earnings.

Diving Into Money

What I like about Aflac is the lack of broken promises. The company's management knows the risks associated with the company's presence in Japan. The Japanese population is declining, like in South Korea, and the demographic risk is real. The size of the market won't grow, and Aflac only focuses on cancer insurance coverage and additional supplemental health coverages. Aware of its own limitations, Aflac decided to maximize the returns to shareholders.

Like Uncle Scrooge, Aflac knows what to do with every last yen. Over the last decade, the company returned about $9 billion through dividend payments.

In addition, the company repurchased massively - almost greedily - its own shares. The outstanding shares reduced from 938.6 million in 2012 to 518.7 million in 2025, or a 44.7% reduction.

Aflac is not a high double-digit grower; it is a cannibal focused on returning excess capital to shareholders.

What Could Go Wrong

Everything is not bright and perfect with Aflac. Even the shiniest duck in the world could suffer from headwinds. I see multiple risks the company faces:

  1. Yen volatility: A large share of earnings comes from Japan. A weaker yen vs. the dollar can reduce reported profits for U.S. investors. The company has tended to mitigate this risk on the investment side by increasing the exposure to the U.S. fixed-income securities. Nonetheless, the core business - the insurance book - remains dependent on yen volatility.
  2. Demographic decline in Japan: I won't reinvent the wheel, as the market is pretty aware of this risk. Japan's population is shrinking and aging. Over time this could limit the growth potential of Aflac's core market. Even worse, the market size could reduce, and the company's core business could generate lower earnings over time.
  3. Regulatory changes: One of the simplest risks to understand but one of the heaviest ones in terms of consequences. Insurance markets are highly regulated, and policyholders tend to be protected by the national regulators. Changes in pricing rules, product approvals, or capital requirements could harm the company's profitability in both markets.
  4. Execution risk in the U.S. segment: The U.S. business is historically more profitable than the Japanese segment. Still, it is a small contributor to earnings. Furthermore, the expense ratio is almost 1.5 to 1.8 times higher than in the Japanese segment. An uncontrolled increase in costs could significantly reduce the U.S segment's profitability.

Like Scrooge fighting against the Beagle Boys and Magica De Spell to protect his Money Bin, Aflac has recurring and persistent enemies: the most notable remains what I would call the "Japan risk" - which includes the foreign exchange volatility, the demographic decline, and the regulatory changes, but also the changes in consumer behavior and the competition from other insurers.

Yet in the Duck universe, Scrooge's real rivals are not thieves or sorcerers but other billionaires competing for the same title. Flintheart Glomgold and John D. Rockerduck have long challenged him for the title of the richest duck in the world. In investing, comparisons work the same way: Value is always relative.

Counting The Coins In The Money Bin

While Glomgold and Rockerduck use nasty tactics to challenge Scrooge, MetLife (MET) and Unum (UNM) are, of course, reputable companies. They do not operate in exactly the same markets, but they can be reasonably considered peers.

And much like their counterparts in the Duck Universe, they stand one or two steps behind Scrooge in terms of valuation.

The relative overvaluation versus peers is easy to explain: Aflac is simply more profitable. Although I don't like focusing on EBIT or EBITDA margins when analyzing insurers, Aflac's ROE and ROA are stronger than MET's and UNUM's.

The duck's outperformance is clearly related to its underwriting discipline and its dedication to maximizing the capital return to shareholders. The real question for investors, however, is not quality - but price.

A Great Duck, But Not A Cheap One

Aflac remains one of the most disciplined insurers in the industry. Its underwriting stability, strong Japanese franchise, and consistent capital returns have built a business that creates value over time for patient and loyal shareholders.

Yet quality rarely comes cheap. Compared with peers such as MetLife and Unum, Mr. Market assigns Aflac a valuation premium, considering it reflects the higher profitability and excess capital management.

At current prices, the stock does not appear expensive, but it also does not offer a material safety margin. For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating.

Like Scrooge McDuck carefully guarding his Money Bin, Aflac has built its wealth patiently. The price simply leaves little room for error.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Price Recovery Makes Sense, Valuation Is Still An Entry Position
data.description:
More often than not, we tend to see a stock as overpriced after its rally. But for high-yield stocks like Dorchester Minerals LP (DMLP), things are not as linear as they seem. After all, it hasn’t yet fully recovered from its one-year downtrend. Its fundamentals and wide mineral rights coverage ensure the sustainability of its capacity and cash distributions. Valuation and technicals are also in sync, prompting me to maintain an optimistic outlook despite the recent overbuying.

DMLP Q4 2025: Challenged But Profits Are Unperturbed

Price volatility has remained intense in the oil and gas market, driven by tensions in Europe and the Middle East and the OPEC oil hike. Before 2025 ended, we saw that oil prices dipped some more, affecting revenues of many businesses in the industry. Even mineral interests or royalty holders like Dorchester Minerals LP were not exempted. Yet, its low-cost business model continued to work in its favor in connection with its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $41.9M ($152.83M - $111.98M), up by 5.4% YoY from $39.7M. I derived these values by getting the difference of their respective annual and three-quarter revenues. This served as its comeback from the weak YoY performance in Q2 and Q3 2025. If we derive each revenue component, nothing special went on during the quarter. In fact, royalties and net profits interest weakened, which should not be surprising. Oil prices weakened during the quarter. So, oil producers and explorers generated lower sales. As a result, the portion of sales or royalties received by the company also decreased.

However, its lease bonus offset the weakness of the other revenue components. This can tell us that DMLP generated more upfront payment from oil E&P companies during the quarter. After all, it increased its leasing capacity after acquiring approximately 3,050 net royalty acres in Adams County, Colorado. This allowed it to cater to more oil and gas companies. There may have been more leasing activity and investment demand as well. Another potential aspect was the policy easing cycle because lower interest rates made financing conditions more stable. This could have helped capital expenditures of oil and gas companies leasing rights from DMLP. As a result, leasing volume and activity picked up as more companies and investors signed more leases and deals.

More interestingly, it remained efficient as its operating costs and expenses only increased by 0.8% despite its increased leasing activity/coverage. This should not be surprising since its business model is not capital intensive. As a result, its net income margin remained high at 38.7% (($57.4M - $41.2M) / $41.9M) versus 35.9% YoY.

Iran Tension

The intensifying Middle East tension is an important consideration for DMLP. Although its core operations are not directly tied to oil prices, its revenues are still tied to the revenues of its lessees. So, oil price volatility has a big spillover into it.

Amid Iran’s attacks and blocking of the Strait of Hormuz, global oil supply concerns continue to push oil prices up. Why? It contributes about 20% to the global oil supply. With that, OPEC’s reserves may be negatively affected. Currently, Brent and WTI are at their 13-month highs of $83.54 and $77.24 per barrel. With that, producing oil may become more profitable for oil E&P companies. This may easily benefit DMLP. After all, its royalty fees are tied directly to revenues or sales of its lessees, not their net income.

On the other hand, this can still be a double-edged sword for DMLP because higher oil prices raise fuel expenses of E&P companies. This can make them rethink their production capacity to avoid overproducing and overselling. Also, higher oil prices can raise the general price level, which can also affect the overall consumption and production. So, we must understand that the impact of the geopolitical tension is not as linear as it seems.

Why I Am Bullish

As an investor here for nearly a year, there are a lot of things to appreciate about DMLP. Its high dividend/distribution yield of 11.3% is just the tip of the iceberg. More importantly, it maintains robust liquidity to sustain its continued mineral rights acquisitions and cash distributions. For instance, the combined amount of its operating and investing cash inflow in 2025 was $136.4M. Meanwhile, it distributed distributions of $136.4M. This led to a negative inflow of $571,000. But because its cash and cash equivalents remained high at $41.9M, DMLP can cover the outflow to sustain its business. It also has accounts receivable of $14.0M, proving that it is still very liquid.

Meanwhile, it has a well-diversified geographical location because it is present in over 20 states and hundreds of countries. Its mineral rights extend from the Permian Basin to other areas, such as the Williston Basin, Appalachia, and the Mid-Continent. And because it does not operate oil wells, DMLP is not capital-intensive. It only acquires new mineral rights if it wishes to expand or spur its growth. But overall, its business is stable and manageable. Additionally, its wide domestic market presence also helps it limit risks related to oil depletion in a single area. But I believe that depletion is still very far from now. Growth may have already peaked, but production is still high.

Aside from oil, natural gas may become a potential growth driver of DMLP. This may be driven by the global data center expansion amid the AI and digital revolution. As a result, power demand is expected to rise in the coming years. In the US alone, power demand is expected to more than double from current levels to 134.4 GW in 2025-2030. Because natural gas remains the primary electricity source, its demand and price may rise in the long run. This may lead to a surge in demand for DMLP’s mineral rights.

Lastly, the policy easing cycle may have some positive spillovers since it can boost economic activity. This can also increase borrower and investor confidence, which can bolster capital spending and investment in oil E&P activities. With that, mineral rights leasing may strengthen this year.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is $3.02 based on its most recent quarterly payout of $0.755. As a result, my expected dividend growth rate in 2026 increased from 0.45% in my previous coverage to 0.72% now. I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to remain conservative and consistent with my previous coverage. But note that the risk-free rate increased to 4.3% in line with the ten-year treasury yield. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00 to be in line with the S&P 500. Using all this information, my new TP, or target price, is $38.88. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $34.10. Hence, DMLP is still cheap at its current price.

Technicals

DMLP’s technicals are still bullish as price rebound attempts continue. It is now above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum has already exceeded the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is now trending upwards, which shows that recent market sentiments have become optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI suggest caution, but the price strength is still evident. The MACD line is still trending above the signal line, which suggests that buying volume is still increasing. The histogram is still within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But also note that the histogram is close to zero or neutral, which shows some selling pressures. Its RSI explains the trend because the stock is already overbought. This may lead to profit-taking and a dip. But considering the trend, I believe that the potential dip or pullback will not lead to a reversal.

Conclusion

Dorchester Minerals LP is still a robust company with a strategic and low-cost business model. Its robust liquidity and increasing domestic coverage allow it to sustain its operations and potential expansion. Its valuation is also still reasonable, which justifies its price rebound. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals remain strong despite recent overbuying. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Beyond The Noise: Why Mastercard's Valuation Now Offers A Rare Margin Of Safety
data.description:
I'll start this article by addressing the elephant in the room. I don't think Mastercard (MA) will be disrupted. Of course, the Citrini report has some interesting points that will need to be monitored in the coming years, such as the issue of agents using other forms of payment.

But the fundamentals still show the complete opposite of disruption, with healthy revenue growth, compounding EPS, etc.

Therefore, I begin my coverage of MA stock with a buy rating.

Mastercard Risks: The Scenario Is Really More Nebulous

Regarding Citrini Research, it's a text that I really liked, and it really brings up some good points, but we have to remember that many parts are an exercise in speculation and not exactly an accurate prediction. Part of the fear that Mastercard will begin to see a loss of growth in 2027 is based on the assumption that in early 2027, “LLM usage had become default” and that many people would be using AI agents without even knowing it. Consequently, this AI agent would choose the most efficient and cheapest payment route, which would take market share away from Mastercard and Visa (V).

In part, this even makes sense. Especially when thinking about B2B (or B2G) and automated payments, perhaps the largest companies will create AI agents to automate these payments/collections, and the faster and cheaper, the better. Indeed, if a Fortune 500 company can save 1% on payment fees, that is already something exceptional.

So there really is a certain risk. In Mastercard's Q4, the payment network grew 9%, and services grew 22% (constant currency). The payment network still seems too solid to lose growth to agents in the coming years, mainly because we are talking about a high volume of cross-border payments, besides the fact that most of it is consumer to business. But the services side may grow a little less with the strong adoption of agents by companies, because these consulting-related services, which help prevent fraud, may become easier to automate and internalize, and that would remove the need to pay high fees to Mastercard. For instance, if Nu Holdings (NU), which is currently a pioneer in tech banking, is investing heavily in innovation, perhaps in a few years its internal system will be able to prevent fraud by detecting and inferring through internal data alone, and then it will no longer need to use third-party services.

Another more sensitive aspect of the Mastercard case, in my view, is the cheaper alternatives, which are not LLMs or AI agents. Being Brazilian, Pix is an example I use constantly, but that's because it was truly revolutionary. It's a bank transfer that you can use at any bank, and it happens instantly and for free. It was truly democratic, and in just a few years it stole a lot of market share from other payment methods.

In the chart below (sorry, couldn't find it in English, but you can find more about Pix here), the light blue line is Pix, and the dark blue line is cards (credit + debit). On the left side, we have the number of transactions, and on the right side, the volume in BRL. Note how Pix has already surpassed cards in terms of quantity, and in terms of volume, it has surpassed them by a lot. Of course, Brazil is still a country that has a long way to go in terms of banking penetration. Many people do not have access to credit, and over time, Mastercard and Visa may continue to accelerate, but today, many people do not use a card when they go to the supermarket. Instead, they point their smartphone at a QR code and pay with Pix. In China, this is also very common, with methods such as WeChat Pay.

For me, that's where the real disruption lies: new methods that are more practical and/or cheaper but that are used by human beings. Where humans can think something like, “I'm not even going to register my card on this website; I'm just going to point my camera at the QR code.”

But Still, Mastercard Has Moats

Even with these uncertainties surrounding new means of payment, I don't think Mastercard will lose ground. Returning to Citrini's case, I believe that LLMs such as Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Gemini will indeed be used for purchases in the near future, such as someone searching for “What is the best laptop to buy for use x and y?” and then the LLM gives some purchase options on Amazon (AMZN). But now, think about how this transaction would be made. In Brazil, perhaps with Pix, but because it is a higher value-added item, credit cards work very well. In the rest of the world, it seems obvious to me that the best option is a card from a brand that people already trust.

I believe that most of the population is not willing to learn how blockchain works - for example, to have a wallet within a blockchain, send USDT to it, and then make these purchases. It is much easier for Mastercard to partner with these agents and LLMs so that purchases can be made with one click on a credit card registered with Amazon, for example, which is much easier and still uses something that the customer already knows and is willing to use.

In other words, I don't think the scenario is entirely clear for Mastercard, but I think its moats protect it well. Even if there is increased competition from other alternatives, I think it will be able to adapt and continue to collect its fees in most cases, not least because it is a very good service with advantages to be used.

We will see how this unfolds in the coming quarters, but so far, the financials are intact. Revenue, as I mentioned, grew well, 18% considering FX, while adjusted expenses grew only 14%, meaning that it is not only the top line that is doing well, but Mastercard, even with already unbelievably high margins, continues to increase and capture operating leverage.

Mastercard Valuation: The Key Point

The key point of my entire article is actually valuation. There are uncertainties in the Mastercard case (as there are in any equity investment), but the market knows this and is already pricing the company with this slightly more uncertain scenario. But as I mentioned, Mastercard is still a quality company and a quality compounder.

And finally, it seems to me that it is possible to pay a good price for a great company. I have been following Mastercard for some time, and it always seems to me that it is a stock that trades very close to a fair valuation. Currently, there is a greater margin of safety.

For example, the average forward P/E is 35x; now it is 26x. That is a 24% discount.

Based on my assumptions, I still think Mastercard offers very attractive shareholder value, and my estimates are even worse than those of the market, which believes that in 2028 the P/E ratio will be 19.8x (also due to buybacks).

Note how the earnings yield will finally reach a more interesting level, such as 6% in 2031. It may not seem like much, but compounding should continue from there on, not to mention that if MA stock returns to trading at 30x forward earnings, it is an implied upside of 80% in about 4-5 years (if at the end of 2030 the market finds it fair for MA stock to trade at 30x forward earnings).

And note that this is still a base case scenario; if LLM is a catalyst for market share rather than a detractor, it could be much better. If services continue to grow, if automation increases, and if margins expand further, these are all positive surprises that could happen and further increase earnings yield in the coming years.

Bottom Line

Mastercard finally seems to me to be at a compelling price. Of course, it is not that big of an asymmetry because even with the more uncertain scenario, the market still knows that this is a quality company with unique fundamentals. I do not think that growth will end because of AI, and I expect that Mastercard will continue to report solid numbers and develop this for shareholders, so my rating is a buy.

data.title: Diversify This
data.description:
The investment sales world loves to talk about the defensive benefit of holding different equity sectors and global markets. The pitch is that there’s always a bull market somewhere, so we can always be buying risk-on products.

The US stock market is heavily concentrated (39%) in the top 10 most expensive companies today (dark blue bar below). No problem, we are urged to ‘diversify’ abroad. The trouble is that other global equity markets are nearly all more concentrated than the US (shown below, courtesy of ISABELNET.com).

Moreover, today, inflated prices are global, with equity valuations at historic highs across all major markets (the 12-month forward price-to-equity multiple, shown below in orange, versus the median for each region over the past 20 years).

In real life, correlations are strongly positive across global markets, particularly in sell-offs. Case in point, all major stock markets except Russia’s (MOEX) have been negative over the past week (in red below). Diversify this.

Protecting capital from the downside of asset bubbles requires more than just different equity marketing wrappers. Crypto isn’t holding up; precious metals and credit are slipping, too. Cash-like equivalents and short- to medium-term individual Treasuries with fixed maturity dates are the most stable allocations - unfortunately, few people hold them in any meaningful weight.

It’s tough to buy low when the masses are maxed out at all-time cycle highs, with little to no cash on hand for buying opportunities that come in bear markets. Poor risk management means the masses often bear capital risk without capturing the promised rewards. In the end, the house gets richer, and individuals end up losing over full market cycles. Rinse and repeat.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: American Healthcare REIT Upgraded As Its Portfolio Grows While Leverage Risk Falls
data.description:
In time for what I've been calling "Follow-up Friday" lately, I focus on REITs again and revisit American Healthcare REIT (AHR), which recently had earnings results on Feb. 26th and is up around +83% since my original coverage in 2024, when I recommended holding on to it.

In subsequent articles, I continued saying hold, including this past December, and some continuing positive elements I liked were portfolio growth and macro forecasts favoring senior care and outpatient facilities. A concern I had was lower EBITDA margins than some peers, as well as a weaker dividend growth case.

For readers new to this stock, AHR operates a diversified portfolio of clinical healthcare real estate across the U.S., U.K., and the Isle of Man, according to its profile, and in today's follow-up, I'll be rating it across 8 rating categories that include both fundamental and technical aspects of this stock.

Thesis Summary

Based on my updated research, I am upgrading this stock to a buy from my prior hold ratings, and the worksheet below shows how my 8 rating categories contributed to the overall score:

Despite having a limited dividend growth history due to only having gone public in 2024, AHR definitely wins in several factors, including continuing macro-level demand for senior care and outpatient services, a robust portfolio growth and development pipeline, improved NOI and FFO growth, a low D/E and real estate portfolio generating positive operating cash flow, positive price forecasts, and technical patterns supporting a bullish view.

Macro & Sector Outlook

In considering what macroeconomic factors can impact this sector AHR is in, I first considered the specific niche this REIT is in within the overall healthcare space, and that is a combination of senior care, skilled nursing, and outpatient facilities in the US and UK, as shown in its portfolio page:

Knowing that, what comes to mind right away is what some forecasts are saying about demand in this niche.

Statista analysts wrote, for instance, that demand for outpatient facilities is driven by "increasing demand for convenient healthcare services, rising patient preference for outpatient procedures, and advancements in medical technology," while JLL wrote in 2025 that "according to Advisory Board, outpatient volumes in the U.S. are expected to grow 10.6% over the next five years."

In terms of senior living, real estate firm Cushman & Wakefield wrote last month:

U.S. senior living property market fundamentals continue to trend in a positive direction, posting 20 straight quarters of stabilized occupancy growth, reaching 90% in the fourth quarter of 2025, the highest level since 2017.

Further, NBC News pointed out last month that "the share of Americans ages 65 and older is projected to reach over 20% by 2035, meaning roughly 1 in 5 Americans may require some form of care," but they also highlighted that a significant portion of this comes not from senior-care facilities but from at-home health aides/nurses, which faces the dual challenge of finding enough qualified professionals but also the labor cost involved.

Although a healthcare REIT as a landlord does not typically manage/operate the facility or deal with patients directly and instead leaves that up to managed operators who run the facility, the above realities highlight not only the ongoing demand for care that exists but also some challenges this niche can face.

I continue to be bullish on the macro environment for this sector, though, given that the upside factors seem to greatly overwhelm some of the challenges mentioned.

Competitive Positioning

In this section, which was not included in my prior few articles on this stock, I take the angle of comparing AHR to 3 peers in the same niche, across a few metrics. The peers I picked are publicly traded REITs with a focus on senior care and outpatient care: Welltower (WELL), Ventas (VTR), and Healthpeak Properties (DOC).

Of these, AHR is the smallest, with a market cap of around $9.9B, while Welltower dominates this group by size. I've covered some of these before, including my recent article on Ventas, and as a REIT investor, I've picked up some shares of Healthpeak too just to wet my beak, and so it is a sector I have become a subject-matter expert in over the last year.

Peer data from Seeking Alpha tells us that AHR has had a respectable 5-year revenue CAGR of +13.7%, 2nd in this group right behind Welltower, and I would point out that its ROE of +2.5% is also mostly in line with Welltower, so AHR certainly has been able to compete in this peer group.

What really sticks out, though, is that over the last 10 years, AHR's price return has beaten that of a major benchmark like the S&P 500, as momentum data shows, and along with Welltower had the best return, indicating considerably strong momentum from investors, despite the S&P 500 being tech-heavy and all the AI-driven bullishness it saw lately.

Beyond these metrics, I think another competitive edge for AHR is that it has penetrated the UK market and therefore is growing beyond just the US, but also is diversified beyond just senior care, as I showed in the last section.

Thus, considering all of these points, I gave AHR a highly bullish view in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Now that we have a sense of the big picture that AHR is in, next let's narrow down to company-specific growth factors and their impact.

For one thing, I like portfolio growth as it can drive future rental flows, thereby directly impacting future upside. We can see from the recent investor deck (pg 16) that AHR has been active in FY25 not just with disposing of some properties but also has seemed to acquire many more in the process:

Further, AHR has a robust pipeline of new developments and property expansions, also highlighted in the same investor deck, and notable is that this capital investment is across many US states:

In the Q4 results press release (pg 2), the company also boasted of having made considerable acquisitions in FY25:

During the year ended December 31, 2025, the Company completed over $950MM of acquisitions across its operating portfolio, including approximately $370.0MM within its ISHC segment and approximately $590.0 million within its SHOP segment. These investments were made in collaboration with the Company’s trusted operating partners.

Complementing this exciting set of data supporting a growth thesis, is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of +13.5%, beating the sector average considerably.

So a strong growth case exists, and thus I am highly bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

The unique nature of REITs also warrants a unique discussion on what impacts profitability/earnings.

In this case, I would compare the EBITDA margins vs peers, since that margin strips away accounting noise and depreciation, giving us a better glimpse of earnings efficiency in the core business:

Like I said in my earlier coverage of this stock, AHR struggles with this margin at only 15.9%, the lowest in this peer group. Additionally, despite some improvement in net margin over the last few years, the EBITDA margin for AHR has not shown a steady improvement trend in this period:

However, another key metric in REITs is the FFO, and AHR saw a +36% YoY FFO growth, which is highly impressive and beats the sector average.

NOI (net operating income) is another key metric in this sector, and notable from headline metrics in the Q4 results is that AHR "achieved total portfolio Same-Store Net Operating Income (“NOI”) growth of 11.8% and 14.2% for the three months and year ended December 31, 2025, respectively, compared to the same periods in 2024."

From another angle, we cannot ignore the analyst consensus on FFO growth, which expects between 11% and 17% YoY FFO growth this year and next, which I think could really keep attracting investor confidence.

Therefore, what supports a modest bullish case here is that despite some weaker EBITDA margins, this REIT has a strong consensus outlook, improved NOI, and FFO growth.

Balance Sheet Risk

The balance sheet story is especially relevant for REITs, as they hold a large portfolio of income-generating assets as well as potentially a large pool of debt.

When it comes to AHR, I would point out that its D/E is 0.50, the lowest in this peer group, along with Welltower, and also, if you notice the trend line (dark blue), it has actually declined over the last year, indicating lower leverage:

While leverage has declined, in the same period operating cash flow grew by +67%, a winning combination, showing that its vast real estate portfolio is a cash-flow-generating engine, considering that balance sheet data shows this REIT is sitting on over $4B in real estate at the end of FY25.

Of course, we should consider asset risk exposure too. This REIT has considerable exposure to an operator called Trilogy, as the graphic below from the investor deck (pg 11) shows, and this indicates the risk needle could move up for this stock if Trilogy were to run into considerable financial difficulties in a sector that is also prone to payor risk and cost inflation, which I've talked about in other articles already.

So while the D/E is low and cash flow has grown, the operator risk remains, and so I am only modestly bullish in this section.

Dividend Outlook

Dividend income has been a major reason why I first got into REIT stocks a few years ago, and so here is the dividend story on AHR in a concise way.

Its fwd dividend yield is nothing remarkable at 1.9%, and actually below the sector average, so it wouldn't be on my list of high-yield stocks right now.

In terms of dividend growth, it has yet to achieve a longer-term proven growth since the stock only went public in 2024, so there is limited data for now to support a strong dividend growth case.

Where it wins for me is AFFO coverage of the dividend. With a quarterly dividend of $0.25/share, consider that the quarterly AFFO/share has continually been above that, from income statement data:

So, it is a low-yield dividend lacking in a proven growth case since it only went public a few years ago, but seems to have adequate AFFO coverage, which supports at least a neutral/hold rating in this section.

Valuation Metrics

In this part, I shift the discussion to the following worksheet I made using Seeking Alpha data on valuation and also forecasting a future share price using the fwd P/FFO multiple and analyst FFO consensus:

In this example, I expect a further +13% upside to the share price, which is actually close to the avg Wall St forecast of +10.4% (as of early Thursday).

This would support a strong buy viewpoint.

Although the stock appears overvalued, I would say the market is paying a premium on its portfolio due to the implied "defensive" nature of healthcare/senior-care vs some other categories like hotels. The market also is rewarding the strong analyst FFO growth consensus and improved NOI, despite this firm having some weaker EBITDA margins.

In other words, a highly bullish view is warranted both due to upside forecasts and elevated valuation that is supported by materially good data rather than just confidence.

Technical Charting

Now we will step aside from fundamental analysis and consider this technical chart, which compares the share price over the last year vs. the 50-day and 200-day simple moving averages, two metrics I love as they smooth out any day-to-day price volatility, especially since my goal here is not a focus on day trading but more on long-term investing.

In this example, there are no classic buy or sell signals such as death cross or golden cross patterns, but rather the 50-day and 200-day SMAs are parallel.

What supports a bullish view here rather than a hold is that both moving averages continue trending upward without breaking, and the 50-day seems to continue providing decent support to the share price. The support was tested this winter, and the share price popped right back up again.

Hence, this technical pattern reinforces the otherwise overwhelmingly bullish tone of my article, rather than challenging it, and boosts this stock's overall score into the buy column.

Risks and Challenge to Thesis

A key risk that can challenge my bullish view, which has not already been included in this rating score, is the fact that the REIT sector is prone to interest rate risk, which is outside its direct control yet can have an impact.

For instance, commercial real estate/CRE lending rates can be impacted by US Treasury yields, so if yields rise, that could make rates go up, impacting both new loans and refinancings at new rates.

In an ideal world, REITs love a low interest-rate environment as it reduces the cost of capital, making it cheaper to borrow both for acquiring or constructing property. Geopolitical events that drive volatility in financial markets can also impact Treasury yields, as well as rates that lenders charge, and as of the start of the week, we know from major media just how volatile things can become quickly.

Conclusion

To conclude my follow-up today, I showed why AHR deserves an upgrade. Key factors are upside drivers supported by portfolio growth along with a favorable macro demand environment for this sector, which the market clearly has already recognized and is pushing this stock higher, without signs that there is a compelling reason not to keep that trend going.

Although interest rate risk exists in the REIT sector, by now I think that also should have already been baked into the share price, so it seems to not be greatly fazing investors right now.

While AHR is not a great dividend idea this month, my bet is on this stock as a capital grower in the long term.

data.title: Nebius: Has My Conviction It Will Be An Outperformer
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The data center and hyperscaler space has been an interesting area to follow and quite controversial sometimes with the numbers and the funding happening there. I’ve read about, as has most, I assume, the circular funding happening here. I find trying to get exposure to the LLM companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) or Anthropic (ANTHRO) might be too risky. Better to go for the renting business, that being Nebius (NBIS), for instance. Buying and then renting the incredibly high-in-demand GPUs from leading GPU manufacturers.

I’ve covered NBIS before, and I continue to have a positive outlook on the stock. It’s incredibly well positioned, and it’s showing strong revenue growth right now. Even if it missed expectations, going from $91.5 million to $529 million in revenues in just 12 months is no small feat. And it’s only growing from here on out as the New Jersey facility continues to move through phases. Management reiterated $7 - 9 billion in annual run rate revenues with now $1.2 billion ARR. For a stock with a market cap of just $23 billion makes the downside look quite limited with these types of growth rates. I’m rating NBIS a Strong Buy again and intend on holding on to my stake for the long run.

How Did My “Strong Buy” Rating Develop?

Since my last article on NBIS, the stock has done quite well after I rated it a Strong Buy. Shares are up some 9.8% at the time of writing this. At one point it was even better, peaking at around $107 per share after it was published. Even with a pretty large miss on both the top and bottom lines, in the last earnings report the stock has continued to do well and showcase some alpha against the S&P 500, which has remained largely flat since mid-December.

Some of the big news that has come out since then (apart from the earnings report) has to be the approval for a 1.2 GW Missouri megaproject. This actually came out a few days ago. It’s considerable because, for those not aware, much of the current investment thesis relied on the 300 MW plant in New Jersey. This new mega project trumps this by a lot and hikes the future revenue and earnings forecast, I think. However, the 1.2 GW plant would still likely need to see more power grid extensions in order to meet the demand, and this as most might know, can take a very long time. I'm looking forward to hearing more comments about this, though, in the next earnings report in late April.

In the industry we saw some pretty volatile days, with CoreWeave (CRWV) announcing they’ll have to double their CapEx plans for 2026 to keep up. This made NBIS take a pretty large hit as well. Comparing both of these companies, which are competitors, their stock prices have moved similarly. CRWV is up 9% since my article on NBIS.

I think that the investment case is still alive and well for NBIS, and I’m sticking with my initial Strong Buy. The market seems to be a little skeptical of AI right now, with many companies seeing their stock prices falling, including some of the largest ones like NVIDIA (NVDA), even after strong quarters and even stronger outlooks. It seems to open up more investment opportunities than anything else. Some correction was to be expected, I think, but then you also need to take advantage of this. The upside of NBIS lies in the broad utilization of AI. It’s not an individual bet per se, but more a bet on that AI will continue to see adoption and greater usage.

The Tavily Acquisition - A Very Logical Purchase

The agentic AI market is projected to grow from approximately $7 billion in 2025 to between $140 billion and $200 billion by the early 2030s, representing a compound annual growth rate exceeding 40%

In mid-February, NBIS acquired a company called Tavily for around $400 million in total, with $275 million being paid in cash upfront. Going by the latest quarterly filing from NBIS, it seems they are well capitalized for this acquisition. They reported $3.6 billion in cash, which is up from $2.4 billion at the 2024 year-end.

I find this quite impressive, as the company has also increased the value of their property and equipment to $5.5 billion, from $846 million a year earlier. As for the acquisition, I think the quote explains everything as to why NBIS would try to enter this market more. That type of growth is not easy to come by.

But what I don’t think was obvious was the fact that this addresses one of the concerns I had in my initial coverage, namely GPU margins. I think by acquiring Tavily, NBIS is directly diversifying their product line. Being just a renting company for B200 chips, which sees depreciating hourly rates over time, is risky and perhaps could carry a certain risk premium as well. By also focusing and investing in agentic AI infrastructure, NBIS is entering a high-margin industry, which could help offset some of the falling margins in the core business.

The synergy comes from the core business of Tavily, which is building search engines for AI agents or LLMs. If someone rents a B200 from NBIS, they could in theory then immediately also plug into the Tavily search API. This is a sort of closed loop, which, after having covered a lot of different companies at this point, I can say almost always carries higher margins with it.

2025 Results Are In, Momentum Hasn’t Gone Anywhere

With NBIS now scaling their New Jersey facility at a fast pace, with an end goal of having it all operational (300 MW) by the end of 2026. From the earnings call, it's clear that the contract with Meta Platforms (META) is now fully serviceable as of early February.

We now expect to have over 2.5 GW of contracted power by the end of 2026, including 800 MW to 1 GW of connected power.

NBIS is then further aiming to get to the 1 GW mark in connected power by 2026 year-end. To get there, we’ve seen a massive raise to the CapEx guidance. This was perhaps to be expected given just how aggressive other hyperscalers have been with their allocations as well.

What I think most people didn't catch is the fact that NBIS is completely booked out. Because of this demand and the fact that even before the company brings capacity online, it’s sold out, contract duration has grown by 50%. That isn't a small thing. This helps provide revenue visibility.

In my risk segment, I’m going to discuss some of the risks that the hiked CapEx planning brings to the table, mostly in the shape of debt and equity financing. But to counter these, I first wanted to mention how NBIS is essentially dollar for dollar protected against this with the $3 billion contract with META and $17.4 billion contract with Microsoft (MSFT). Even though it's over several years of revenues being recognized, I think it still helps improve the current financial image of the business. But of course, the cash flow statement won't look that great because it will be weighed heavily down by the CapEx spending this year, without necessarily seeing the net income at the top to balance this out.

Because of this binding and assurance of future revenues, the 2026 $20 billion CapEx plan, I think, doesn't look that crazy anymore. It’s essential to even being able to serve this revenue in the future and meet the contract obligations. At least that’s how I see it. These are high-growth stocks.

When the headlines said NBIS missed the Wall Street revenue and earnings expectations, most, I think, missed the fact that NBIS posted a positive adjusted EBITDA for the first time in a long time. It grew from a negative $63.9 million in Q4 2024 to a now positive $15 million. Still, 2025 full-year results were negative; it seems that going into 2026, results will be positive, though.

On adjusted EBITDA, we expect group adjusted EBITDA margin to be approximately 40% for 2026.

With this guidance and assuming a midpoint ARR of $8 billion, NBIS would be generating in the range of $3.2 billion in adjusted EBITDA. For a company with an EV of $24.2 billion right now, it means I’m looking at an EV/EBITDA of 7.56x. Currently the sector median is at 13.65x, meaning NBIS could be trading at a near 50% discount based on this metric alone. But because of the additional debt that would land on the balance sheet of NBIS, we need to recalculate here to adjust for future EV instead. In the risk segment, I'm including a quote saying that NBIS has 60% of the capital out of the $20 billion required in CapEx; this leaves $8 billion at the top that needs to be funded. We could basically add on this figure to the $24.2 billion EV figure at the time of writing this. This gets me to $32.2 billion. Divide this by the EBITDA, and the multiple goes to 10.06x instead. Still, to me this looks like a quite attractive valuation to buy at considering the future growth of the business I see.

Of course, adjusted EBITDA can be controversial, because, well, it’s adjusted. Adding things like SBC and other one-time expenses does inflate the figure.

Normal EBITDA accounting, of course, takes net income and adds back interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. When doing this, the last quarter showed instead a negative EBITDA of $41.5 million. The bridge to the adjusted EBITDA comes from restructuring and SBC expenses.

The positive part comes when we actually look at the SBC figure. It has dropped off sharply from $37.6 million in Q4 2024 to now $24.9 million in the last quarter. Even when we strip away certain costs, the core business of NBIS is still showing a lot of improvements here, I think.

The other sign I want to point towards to show operating leverage here is the fact that ARR was able to hit $1.25 billion, but the SBC dropped by 34% YoY. To me this shows that NBIS no longer needs to aggressively hire to expand its business. This is true operating leverage for me.

Risks

In order to capture the large and growing opportunity that we see for the future, we plan to invest in CapEx in the range of $16 billion to $20 billion in 2026. We already have about 60% of the capital needed for this range from our balance sheet, existing operations and commitments.

One of the biggest risks I see right now is the mismatch between required CapEx and the existing capital on the balance sheet. As was pointed out in the Q4 earnings call, there is only a 60% coverage. The company said quite clearly they are going with both debt and equity funding. Non-current debt levels sit at $4.1 billion, costing $57.8 million in annual interest expenses. This comes out to a 1.4% cost of debt, which is incredibly low. I can see the company taking on much more debt if they can manage to finance it at these low levels. But I do expect there to be a level of dilution as well. With the amount needed, raising about $1 billion would equate to about 12.6 million in share dilution. I don't think this is farfetched to see in the coming months. This would raise the share count by 4.9%.

If, instead, though, NBIS decides to only fund through equity, the $8 billion needed on the high end would equate to a dilution rate of about 39.8%. This is quite high and would most definitely carry with it a larger risk premium to the stock price as well. If the industry starts seeing more risk and more defaults or deals falling through, credit lenders might be hesitant to lend the sort of money that NBIS might need. This could force the company to go down the route of equity financing instead, which, as I've just outlined, can have severe effects on the stock price, even in just the near term, honestly.

The second risk is the customer concentration. NBIS right now works with META and MSFT. That is basically it. MSFT's strategy seems to be to work with companies like NBIS rather than directly invest in and operate the data centers to get risk off the table. This means that if the market turns sour, it has arguably much more negotiating power than NBIS has. This could pressure both revenues and earnings for NBIS, and what might look fruitful in terms of outlooks, might quickly turn cloudy. If this happens, I expect there to be a sizable risk premium attached to the price.

The Full Picture

NBIS continues to perform well, and the massive revenue surge it had in the last quarter shows this very thing. The company continues to scale and seems on track to hit over $7 billion in ARR by the 2026 year-end. For a company with a market cap of just $23 billion, I think this makes it one of the more fairly valued stocks in the hyperscaler industry right now. It’s not particularly overvalued and has a strong balance sheet with a growing cash pile.

It might lack some of the funding for the 2026 CapEx plan it has guided for, but given the low cost of debt, it seems that NBIS has relatively easy access to “cheap capital” right now, which makes this less of an issue. Just like I said in December, this is one of the best hyperscalers to buy right now, and my conviction hasn’t shifted since then. Rating NBIS a Strong Buy.

data.title: Short Selling And Put Buying Still Point To Big Tech Rally
data.description:
This is an update of the article published on January 29th titled: Short Selling And Put Buying Point To Big Tech Rally. Since we wrote the article, the S&P 500 is down 1.5%.

The Time Frame

Knowing the investment time frame is important, so we're starting this article with a clarifying statement about time frames.

The Sentiment Kingis long-term oriented (bull and bear markets). We adjust long-term allocations if our intermediate-term indicators (ST-MSI) suggest it. We have no interest in the short term, except as it can help understand intermediate and long-term trends. In our world, one month is short-term.

We Measure "Fear" And "Greed"

Warren Buffett said, “A simple rule dictates my buying. Be fearful when others are greedy and be greedy when others are fearful.” Notice he didn’t say “interest rates” or “earnings,” just “emotions.” This was his way of stating the theory of contrary opinion, which is a theory used by every great investor of the last 100 years. He didn’t, however, provide ways to monitor or measure fear or greed.

The overall subject is called “investor sentiment” or “investor expectations.” This article will show you two different ways to measure what investors expect for the direction of stock prices. In the process we will update the indicators presented in the first article.

Short Selling

From 1940 until 1985, short selling indicators were the primary tool to measure bearish investor expectations. It worked as a contrary opinion indicator. Large amounts of short selling occurred right before prices were ready to rise. This was the opposite of what you would want.

In 2010 ProShares created four inverse ETFs that shorted the market 1X times. This allowed serious long-term investors to easily short the market. This chart shows combined daily buying activity in the four 1X short funds.

We found that investor activity in these four funds worked in the same contrary way: investors usually bought short funds in high numbers right before prices were about to rise. We've highlighted that with black arrows. Current buying levels in these short funds equal that of last April's lows.

Notice we've marked on this chart 1, 2, and C. You'll see the same numbers and letter on the next chart. We'll explain what these mean in the section “Flat Corrections” below.

Heavy Directional Put Buying in Technology Stocks

This second chart shows directional put buying in all 661 technology stocks plotted against XLK, which is the iShares technology ETF. The CBOE defines “directional put buying” as all put transactions executed at the asking price. Put buying in all 661 has surged and is currently 2X above the level of buying at the April lows.

This indicator acts as a contrary opinion indicator too and confirms the short selling signal of the previous chart.

Notice that we've marked the same 1, 2, and C on this chart as we did on the previous chart. We've also drawn a red arrow, which is essentially our forecast of what these indicators are saying.

What do these refer to?

A “Flat” Correction: A Strong Bullish Pattern

A flat is a type of correction that occurs after a powerful advance. It’s a sign of underlying market strength.

Most investors think of a correction as a price sell off. But you can also have a time correction, where prices effectively move sideways, creating a flat adjustment period.

Once finished, the correction becomes a high level platform to launch another strong advance. It's shown by the diagram below. The 1, 2, and C in the previous two charts refer to the 1, 2, and C in this diagram of a flat.

With the S&P 500, wave 1 went from April to November and was a 38% price gain. So far, after a four-month correction, the S&P has lost 1%.

When Will Wave 2 Begin?

This is the $64,000 question. While we've pointed out the bullish implications of high short selling and put buying for the last two months, we haven't recommended to our subscribers that they act. That's because, while these indicators are very useful, they are not the primary indicators we use to make final decisions. Those are our two master sentiment indicators. One - the ST-MSI – we update for subscribers every day.

Corrections don't last forever. After four months, we're getting close. When we do get a signal, we'll notify you after a few weeks. Obviously, our subscribers get notification immediately.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Value ESG Portfolios Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities finished a strong 2025 albeit with more subdued gains in the fourth quarter, with the S&P 500 Index rising 2.7% during the period to end the year up 17.9%. Value stocks outpaced growth in the quarter across all market caps as the market pivoted from recent high-momentum AI winners and leadership broadened. The benchmark Russell 1000 Value Index gained 3.8% for the quarter, capping off a 15.9% return for 2025.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in financials and health care, underweights to real estate and consumer staples and a health care overweight were beneficial.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the IT, materials, consumer discretionary and industrials sectors detracted.
  • Outlook: Thanks to our long duration, we see the recent selloff in high-quality businesses as a significant opportunity, and we have recently initiated or added to positions in several at attractive valuations.

Performance Review

  • During the fourth quarter, quality stocks across the market cap spectrum underperformed the broader market by the widest margin since the late 1990s. This was a headwind to the Strategy, which trailed the Russell 1000 Value in the quarter as several high-quality long-term holdings sold off.
  • Within information technology (IT), long-term portfolio holding Motorola Solutions (MSI) sold off as its revenue growth rate continued to normalize in its core land mobile radio (LMR) devices business; the company is working off an unusually high backlog created by COVID-era government funding programs. While its LMR devices business is slowing, we think the company has augmented its core franchise with faster-growing adjacencies and increased its mix of software and services. We think Motorola Solutions is a core holding for our Strategy that offers attractive risk-reward at current valuation given a sustainable mid-single-digit top-line growth rate with continuously improving profit margins.
  • Our high-quality bias applies across the portfolio, including areas of AI. We are more exposed to higher-quality AI exposed stocks such as Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Microsoft (MSFT), which lagged this year (although the first two were both up more than 40%) relative to lower-quality more commodity-exposed AI stocks like Micron (MU), Sandisk and Lumentum (LITE). While these lower-quality companies are likely to benefit more from supply chain constraints in the near term, we believe that businesses with more sustainable moats are likely to deliver superior returns through the cycle for investors.
  • Strength in consumer spending from the third quarter looked to carry over into the holiday season, helping Capital One (COF) and American Express (AXP). Capital One reported a large earnings beat driven by lower credit costs and a large reserve release helped by lower charge-offs. The quarter showed very strong operating results from Capital One in its first full quarter following its acquisition of Discovery (DFS). The icing on the cake was the announcement of a $16 billion buyback authorization (11%–12% of the market cap) following management's review of post-acquisition capital requirements. American Express also topped earnings estimates and raised the lower end of its guidance helped by strong spending and a bounce back in travel. Its refresh on its Platinum cards is also being well-received.
  • A fourth-quarter rotation to health care driven by a decline in policy uncertainty, improved earnings, attractive valuations and a pickup in M&A activity helped our holdings in the sector.
  • Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), a supplier of life science tools and diagnostics, delivered strong earnings driven by its analytical instruments segment and strong operating margins. The company has also benefited from improving results in its growing bioprocessing business and raised revenue and EPS guidance.
  • Strength in its oncology segment helped AstraZeneca (AZN) deliver stronger-than-expected third-quarter results in November and reaffirm its guidance.

Outlook

  • Our core tenet is to invest in high-quality businesses with underappreciated, sustainable, durable edges, and we remain convinced that this is the path to superior returns through the cycle. In the current frothy market, however, this style has been under pressure. Thanks to our long duration, we see the recent selloff in high-quality businesses as a significant opportunity, and we have recently initiated or added to positions in several — such as Linde (LIN), Parker-Hannifin (PH) and WEC Energy (WEC) — at attractive valuations. We believe the shifting market regime offers significant value opportunities, especially as overall market breadth improves and capital flows shift away from high-flying growth stocks toward overlooked, high-quality businesses.

References

  1. *Cumulative total returns

ClearBridge Large Cap Value ESG Portfolios

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value. Please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights for the latest performance figures. Investors cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Fees: Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite's investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. "Pure" gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or "true") gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of-fee return may include a blend of "true" gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and "pure" gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model "wrap fee" (3.0% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for equity and balanced portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

What are the Risks?

There is no guarantee that the strategy's ESG directives will be successful or will result in better performance.

Glossary

A buyback is the repurchase of outstanding shares (repurchase) by a company in order to reduce the number of shares on the market.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in interest rates.

Earnings per share (EPS) is defined as a company's net income divided by the number of outstanding shares of its common stock.

Moat is an advantage a company has which allows it to protect its market share and profitability.

data.title: China Lowers Growth Target As Geopolitical Risk Complicates Outlook
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Two Sessions to be key for this year’s policy backdrop

China's annual target-setting is always an important event. Since GDP growth targets were first published in 1990, China has fallen short of the target only a couple of times. Generally, betting on China to miss its target has been a losing bet for forecasters.

This year's GDP growth target was reduced to 4.5-5.0%, a slight softening from the more ambiguous "around 5%" target set in the past three years. While it was debatable how much flexibility "around 5%" entailed, most market participants viewed this as within 0.2-0.3pp of 5%. With the new target, there appears to be a tolerance for slower growth, which should give policymakers more flexibility to pursue quality growth, a priority in recent years.

The government work report outlined an intention for "laying a solid foundation for doubling per capita GDP by 2035 compared to 2020," a key goal set by President Xi in the past.

The softer GDP target was in line with our expectations, as we had hints of this outcome earlier when various provinces also revised growth targets lower. Our GDP forecast for the year is 4.6% year-on-year, which would fall within this range.

Anti-involution push continues in the background

The government recently published "Price Behaviour Compliance Guidelines for the Automotive Industry", taking aim at automakers pricing cars below cost to gain market share. It also signalled a crackdown on price fixing. Much like the regulations introduced for platform companies in 2025, new guidance was issued to prevent major brands from pressuring dealers to sell certain models at prices that would leave them unprofitable.

The anti-involution measures have attracted considerable attention and debate over the past year. It can be argued that these measures are already beginning to show some effect. PMI and PPI data show that ex-factory price indicators have been trending mostly higher in recent months.

Middle East uncertainty a potential risk to outlook

With the escalation in the Middle East, China, the world’s largest oil importer, could be negatively impacted, particularly if the situation continues to worsen. China’s official data shows zero crude oil imports from Iran, but widely cited third-party data estimates around 1.38m barrels per day of imports. This would be around 12% of China’s total imports of 11.55m barrels per day. It’s estimated that more than 40% of China’s oil imports go through the Strait of Hormuz. If the Strait remains blocked for an extended period, China may need to tap its strategic petroleum reserves.

If oil prices continue to rise for an extended period, the developments could lead to greater inflationary pressure in China this year. This could finally drive PPI inflation back into positive territory and the CPI over 1%.

China has a modest level of investment exposure to Iran, with around $4.5bn in cumulative outbound direct investment (ODI) as of the end of 2024. In 2025, we saw around $643m, or 0.4% of outward direct investments, going to the country. It’s unclear how these investments might be affected by recent events. The impact, though, is quite limited when considering China’s broader portfolio of outward investment.

We’re in wait-and-see mode on the potential impact on growth and inflation. For now, risks are balanced to the upside for inflation, while the impact on growth is expected to be limited. The domestic policy and external demand outlooks should be more important factors in the growth outlook.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
